entry.title: Re: SAP Build Process Automation – Click Dynamic "Clear" Button Based on Journal Entry
entry.content: I had a similar problem and solved it by filtering the entries first so that only the one entry which I want to process is displayed. Then I can perform the action I want (in your case click the clear button) always on the first line. So try entering your Journal Entry Number in the the search field and press the search icon. Then you can press clear on the first line.

entry.title: HCM Brasil: pré-requisitos de Support Package para implementação de SAP Notes – 2027/2028
entry.content:
Olá pessoal,

Como todos os anos, é hora de planejar a atualização dos Support Packages do seu sistema HCM Brasil.

A atualização anual dos Support Packages para HCM Brasil é obrigatória e fundamental para manter seu sistema funcionando sem problemas e em conformidade com as leis e regulamentações.

Manter seu sistema atualizado traz uma série de vantagens, incluindo:

  • Acesso às novas funcionalidades e melhorias: As atualizações de Support Packages trazem novas funcionalidades, correções de erros e otimizações de desempenho, garantindo a melhor experiência possível. Também traz correções de vulnerabilidades de segurança, protegendo seu sistema contra ameaças e garantindo a confidencialidade dos seus dados.
  • Compatibilidade com as SAP Notes: A atualização garante que você possa implementar as SAP Notes, que contêm correções importantes, atualizações legais e melhorias de segurança. A partir de Maio de 2027, a aplicação de novas notas para os componentes SAP_HRCBR e S4HCMCBR exigirá Support Packages da versão 2025 ou superior.
  • Prevenção de problemas: Manter seu sistema atualizado ajuda a evitar potenciais problemas de compatibilidade e garante a estabilidade das suas operações.

IMPORTANTE: A partir de Maio de 2027, sistemas com Support Packages anteriores a Maio/2025 não poderão implementar SAP Notes referentes aos componentes SAP_HRCBR e S4HCMCBR. Isso significa que você não receberá correções, novas funcionalidades e, principalmente, atualizações legais, colocando suas operações em risco.

A tabela abaixo detalha os níveis mínimos de Support Package para 2027/2028:

Para mais detalhes, recomendações e informações específicas sobre cada componente, acesse nosso WorkZone.

Próximos passos:

Não deixe para a última hora! Recomendamos que você inicie o planejamento da sua atualização o quanto antes.

Compartilhe este importante aviso com seus colegas e siga a tag HCM Payroll Brazil na SAP Community para se manter atualizado.

Fiquem à vontade para deixar um feedback, comentário ou pergunta sobre este Blogpost no espaço abaixo.

Atenciosamente,

Anderson Marques

entry.title: #COE#RFL01- Change company code currency from EUR to VND
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community, 

When creating our company code the VND company currency was not available in SAP Cloud Public 2 SL.

So we created Company XX , that was set up with the € as its company currency.

We decided to use VND as the transaction currency instead of the company currency, but entries in euro currency like balance carryforward, are being generated, which makes it difficult to use the transaction currency.

Since SAP now supports the VND  currency., we're wondering if SAP offers a solution for customers who had to find workarounds because the VND currency wasn't available in the system then?

Do you know if there's a way to switch to the VND currency, or do we have no choice but to create a new company in VND?

If you’ve encountered this type of situation, please feel free to share your advice, tips, and solutions.

Thank you all !

entry.title: Re: SAP Build Process automation: Capturing SAP GUI 8.00 Screen fails
entry.content:
In the meantime I found that the Error "The screen could not be captured. Relaunch the recorder" is in indeed caused by the parameter settings :
https://help.sap.com/docs/build-process-automation/sap-build-process-automation/enabling-scripting-on-server-side-bff7ad3f1ee44c909a5daa8173dc9eae?locale=en-US
In systems in which the parameters are set "correctly", I could capture perfectly fine.

Please note that there are more parameters in addition to sapgui/user_scripting which can set the authoritions on a more detailed level. For example sapgui/user_scripting_per_user makes it possible to enable the captures for users individually, user need authorization S_SCR for recording.

  • sapgui/user_scripting_disable_recording
  • sapgui/user_scripting_set_readonly
  • sapgui/user_scripting_per_user

Also check KBA 2296251 
I just wanted to document this in case someone stumbles on this issue as well.

I have configured the MCP server in GitHub Copilot as follows:

{

    "adt-mcp-server": {
      "url": "http://localhost:2234/mcp",
      "requestInit": {
        "headers": {

Interestingly, yesterday the MCP server was working and I could see all the exposed ADT MCP tools in GitHub Copilot. Today the same configuration is failing during initialization.

At the same time, GitHub Copilot is reporting errors such as:

Failed to fetch models from CAPI (HTTP 403)
Failed to initialize models

I am currently checking with our GitHub administrators regarding Copilot Chat/model access.

Could the MCP server initialization failure be related to the fact that my GitHub Copilot account does not currently have access to a Copilot model/LLM? Or should the ADT MCP tools still be discoverable independently of Copilot model access?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Kind regards,

Sanjeev Jain

entry.title: Navigating the 5-Country Strategy with SAP Central Business Configuration to Speed Up Implementation
entry.content:
Co-Author: Jyoti Nagar 

You’re using SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and you're expanding. Your project is going global, reaching new markets and new customers. It’s an exciting time, but with rapid growth comes the need for careful planning. To ensure your expansion is smooth and to have consistent project momentum, there’s a 5-country strategy to help you plan effectively.

We’re referring to the 5-country delta guideline for managing changes in SAP Central Business Configuration. This isn't just a piece of technical trivia; it's a fundamental system rule designed to ensure stability. Understanding it will save you from project delays, support tickets, and deployment headaches.

What is the 5-Country Delta Guideline?

The rule is simple: Any system change in SAP Central Business Configuration, such as a scope extension (including country additions), cannot result in a difference (a ‘delta’) of more than five countries.

If you try to perform an action that exceeds this established guideline, the system will automatically block it. Unblocking requires manual intervention from the SAP Support team, which can halt your progress.

Let's break down the two critical scenarios where this approach comes into play.

Scenario 1: The Standard Scope Extension

This is the most straightforward case. You have a project live in a set number of countries, and you want to add more.

Imagine your project is live in 10 countries.

  • ✅ GOOD: You request to add 4 new countries. The delta is +4. This aligns with the delta guideline, and the system will process it automatically. Your rollout continues smoothly.
  • ❌ BLOCKED: You request to add 6 new countries all at once. This goes beyond the guideline, so the request cannot be processed automatically until SAP Support can intervene.

The Pro Tip: For large rollouts, plan in phases. If you need to launch in 12 new countries, structure it as three separate, manageable requests:

  • Phase 1: Add 5 countries.
  • Phase 2: Add another 5 countries.
  • Phase 3: Add the final 2 countries.

Scenario 2: Important Consideration in Merging and Rebasing (Main Line vs. Parallel Line)

One important scenario that can catch teams off guard involves merging and rebasing between the main line and a parallel line – especially after the introduction of the delta threshold.

The 5-country approach doesn't just apply to adding new countries; it also applies to the difference in country scope between the main line and the parallel line when you try to merge or rebase them.

This is particularly relevant for existing customers whose implementation was already in progress before the delta threshold was enforced. Previously, the delta threshold was open-ended, enabling customers to include as many countries as needed.

New customers are not affected in the same way, as the system now applies this rule from the outset – ensuring that no country delta exceeds 5.

Let's look at a common example:

  • At the time of branching, the main line and the parallel line were fully aligned and in sync, covering the same initial scope (for example, 3 countries: DE, IN, US).
  • Over time, the scope started to evolve differently:
  • The main line remained the same, with the original 3 countries in scope: DE, IN, US
  • The parallel line expanded significantly, having 9 countries in total now (added in multiple waves): DE, IN, US, CA, AU, GB, BR, FR, CN
  • As a result, the scope difference between the two lines is 6 countries (9 − 3 = 6): CA, AU, GB, BR, FR, CN

For a period of time, everything may appear to function normally. However, when you attempt to rebase the parallel line or merge changes back into the main line, the system evaluates this as a single action. It's trying to resolve a country delta of 6, which is greater than 5.

Result: The Rebase/Merge operation is automatically blocked.

Your project activities are blocked, and you need to create a support case to get the process untangled.

Your Action Plan: How to Plan for Success

This approach isn't here to slow you down. It's here to protect the integrity of your projects. By building your process around it, you can ensure you never get blocked.

  1. Plan Phased Rollouts for New Countries. This is the most important takeaway. If you're going big, think in batches of five or fewer. Build these phases into your project roadmap from day one.
  2. Keep Your Branches in Sync. For teams using the main line and the parallel line, don't let the country scope drift too far apart. Regularly rebase or merge to keep the country delta small and manageable.
  3. Communicate Across Teams. Make sure everyone – from project managers to developers to deployment specialists – understands this rule. When this guideline isn’t considered, it can occasionally lead to delays in a major release.

By keeping the 5-country delta approach in mind, you can take full control of your global expansion, ensuring your project timelines are met without any unwelcome surprises. Plan smart and expand smoothly.

Did you like this blog? If so, please leave us a thumbs up at the top. Also, make sure to follow the SAP Central Business Configuration tag or follow this dedicated blog collection to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

entry.title: External Revenue Recognition Key Derivation for Project-Based Sales
entry.content:
With CFD 2602.3, SAP introduces External Revenue Recognition Key Derivation, enhancing how revenue recognition keys are handled in project-based sales scenarios.

This capability is part of the ongoing evolution of Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR) and improves the integration between:

  • Project-Based Sales (EPPM)
  • Sales & Distribution (SD)
  • Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR)

Previous Behaviour

Before CFD 2602.3, the system followed strict rules:

  • The revenue recognition key derived during sales order processing had to exactly match the revenue recognition key on the WBS element
  • If the keys did not match → sales order could not be saved
  • If no key existed → system attempted to derive one from the system configuration

What’s New in CFD 2602.3

The new feature introduces a configurable parameter: External Revenue Recognition Key Derivation

  • The revenue recognition key stored on the WBS element is always considered the correct value.
  • When the parameter is set, the system no longer checks whether the revenue recognition key stored on the WBS element matches the key derived during the sales order process.

 

Configuration Options

The new parameter supports three modes:

1. Default (Empty)

  • System behaves exactly as before
  • Ensures full backward compatibility

2. External Derivation – Replace (01)

  • No consistency check between derived and stored recognition keys is performed.
  • If the recognition key on the EPPM project (WBS element) is empty, the system derives the key from the derivation table.
  • If the recognition key on the EPPM project (WBS element) is already maintained, it remains unchanged.

 3. External Derivation – Error (02)

  • No consistency check between derived and stored recognition keys is performed.
  • If the recognition key on the EPPM project (WBS element) is empty, the system returns an error when the project is assigned to a sales order item.
  • If the recognition key on the EPPM project (WBS element) is already maintained, it remains unchanged.

Process Flow

entry.title: Live Data Connectivity in SAP Analytics Cloud - Overview and Direction
entry.content:
Over the past year and a half we have been quietly introducing and expanding a new way to connect to data in SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) and in this blog post I'm going to explain a little bit about why we're doing this, how it works and what the future of it looks like.

For as long as SAC has existed we have had the "Live Data Model" button in the modeler. This has been our traditional way of connecting to live data and it works via connecting to existing SAP Business Warehouse (BW) queries, S/4 HANA views, and SAP HANA calc views. This allows customers who have existing data and semantic models in their on their warehouse systems and HANA databases to reuse those existing semantic models in the context of analytics cloud. Some customers have BW queries that have been around for almost 2 decades and have used them with various client tools over the years, be they BEx Analyzer, Analysis Office, Webi, Lumira, and now SAC. That semantic content is safe and can continue to be used with each new generations of tools. This is also how the SAP Datasphere (DSP) SQL views and Analytic Models work.

Lately, we've been introducing a new way of connecting to data that's not necessarily so obvious, but has enormous impact; the live data connection. The way this works is that you start creating a normal SAC model and simply say opt to create a new model from data; the "I need to create a model from data, so that I can craft my semantic model around my data" option. If the data source that you choose supports live data, you're given the option to import the data or to consume it live. In a nutshell we are taking advantage of the Smart Data Access (SDA) in SAP HANA, to use virtual tables as the basis of the fact table (aka data foundation) and take care of setting up the fact table type for you, behind the scenes.  This approach forms the basis for both the live data connection and  Seamless Planning. 

That’s it! There’s no magic, and the best part is you don’t have to decide at the very beginning of the workflow whether you want to create a model with imported data or with live data. Federated data is not just for data engineers anymore!

In the context of planning, we use the term "live versions." This is because planning models can have multiple versions of fact data.  In fact, you can add a live version to an existing planning model. Sometimes also colloquially we refer to the live data connection as "SQL live" even though that is a misnomer, because not all supported data sources use SQL interfaces.

Who classic Live Data Models are for and when

• Users who have existing semantic models in S/4, BW, or HANA (e.g., BW queries or HANA calc views) that they want to re-use.

SAC acts as a runtime client in this scenario and the semantic model is a black box from SAC’s perspective, so if you want to control the semantic model from the SAC side, this is not the best choice.

Who Live Data Connectivity is for and when

• Users building analytic models, when they want to ensure that the remote data source is the single source of truth.
• Planners, when they don’t want to replicate data into the actuals version, especially when that source is a large data lake.  This is also how Seamless Planning works.
• Others who don’t want the complexity of scheduling load jobs. For example, suppose you have a shared Excel file where several users are collectively editing quarterly risk analysis. If you wanted a risk analysis dashboard based on that data, you might find it very convenient if that dashboard is always based on the current values in the Excel table.

Why we're building out the live data connection

The traditional Analytics Cloud data model required you to replicate or import your data into SAC. In some respects, this is very simple. You simply connect to the data, copy it into Analytics Cloud, and then you build your semantic model on top of that for use in Analytics Cloud. If you want to update this data later, then you can always set up and schedule a load job to periodically update the data. You can bring data in from multiple sources into the same semantic model. Etcetera, etcetera.

But… We run into certain limitations in certain scenarios. For example:

• A customer may have a very large data lake. They want to do planning based on the data in this data lake, but don’t want to replicate gigabytes of data into SAC.
• Some sources are updated on a continuous basis and the replicated data might not reflect the real-time state.
• Some users may not want to be bothered with the additional effort of scheduling updates. Sure, scheduling load jobs in SAC is a far cry from data engineering, but it is still cognitive load and effort that brings minimal value in some situations.

For such situations, live data connections are a clear quality-of-life improvement.


Wait! What about DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy?

DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy are complementary to live data connections. Think of live data connections as "Federation Light." It does not require a data engineer skill set, nor does it require your SAC tenant to be in a BDC seamless planning formation. It augments DSP’s Remote Tables and BDC Zero Copy and does not replace the data engineer toolchain.

 

Which Connections are supported for live

As of mid-2026, live data connectivity is supported for:
• Google BigQuery - was the first data source enabled with this capability.
• Microsoft Azure SQL Database - added in quarterly release (QRC) 2026.Q2.
• Snowflake - added in QRC 2026.Q1.

Support for SharePoint Lists and Excel files in SharePoint are planned for QRC 2026.Q4. Other sources—especially the popular cloud data lakes—are planned to follow.

Authentication

Authentication to the remote data source is currently only supported for a service or technical user, as is the case with DSP remote tables. This means that if you need to use record level data access rules from the original source, you will have to replicate the access tables in the source into SAC data access controls (DAC). This is the same behavior that we have in DSP today. We are currently working on enabling user propagation to the remote source, which would allow you to use the remote data access tables with live connectivity in SAC without replicating your DAC. This will be a centralized BDC service for both SAC and DSP, though it will be available first in SAC. It is not on the Roadmap Explorer yet, but when we have a clearer idea of the release quarter, we will publish it.


Getting Started with Live Data Connections

To get started with live data connections, you want to ensure that your SAC tenant is on HANA Cloud and that live data connections are enabled on your tenant.

You can find the HANA version by checking the About page. You can find the About page by locating your user profile button, clicking it, and then selecting About at the bottom of the popup. The HANA version will be displayed on the About page. If your HANA version is not yet HANA Cloud, be advised that we are in the midst of migrating customer tenants over and it should be available in the course of the next few months, but you can also check with your account team to see when your tenant will be migrated.

Next, make sure that live data connections are enabled on your tenant. Doing this will enable SAC models to use live data when the data source supports it. To do this, go to the Administration module, select the Data Source Configuration tab, and ensure that Enable Data Access Agent is toggled on. This will enable the microservice that enables live data connectivity for most sources.

Now you are ready to set up the data sources in the Connections module.

Important note about connections that use the Cloud Agent or Open Connectors for import connectivity

Any source using a JDBC connection via the Cloud Agent or Open Connectors for import connectivity will behave a little bit differently between live and imported. This is because the import connection runs over the Cloud Agent via JDBC, or via BTP Open Connectors, while the live connection runs directly from SAC to the data source, cloud-to-cloud.

For example, if you have configured your Cloud Agent for import but not enabled the Enable Data Access Agent option, then you will only see the option to import data. Conversely, if you have not configured JDBC import via the Cloud Agent but have toggled Enable Data Access Agent on, then you will only have live connectivity. Also, the non-JDBC (live) connection needs its own entry in the Connections module. The tenant that I took the screenshots from does not have a Cloud Agent connected to it, but it does have live connectivity enabled and a Snowflake driver and an Azure SQL driver enabled. Therefore, on my tenant I only see the live option.

This behavior currently affects Snowflake and Microsoft Azure SQL Database connectivity. Google BigQuery is cloud-to-cloud for both import and live connectivity and is not affected. The legacy Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection runs over Open Connectors, so the upcoming Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection will have this behavior, but only temporarily. It is not in the Roadmap Explorer yet, but by mid-2027 we plan to release a new, better Microsoft SharePoint Excel connection that does not use Open Connectors. There is no legacy Microsoft SharePoint Lists connection for import, so until we can add the import option, it will be live only.

Useful Links

KBA 2928379

Configuring a Google BigQuery live connection

Configuring a Microsoft Azure SQL Database live connection

Configuring a Snowflake live connection

An excellent (external) blog post by Rafael Sachs about using Snowflake as a live source in SAC

entry.title: Driving PPWR Compliance with SAP Responsible Design and Production: What’s New in the Latest Release
entry.content:
The Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation (PPWR) is rapidly moving from policy to reality, with enforcement deadlines approaching in August 2026. For companies placing products on the EU market, compliance is no longer optional—it’s business-critical.

With the latest updates in SAP Responsible Design and Production, SAP is delivering powerful new capabilities to help customers stay ahead of PPWR requirements and transform compliance into a strategic advantage.

What’s New in the Latest PPWR Release

The latest RDP release introduces an integrated set of capabilities that enable end‑to‑end PPWR compliance.

1. Extended Packaging Data Model

SAP has enhanced the packaging data model to cover:

  • Packaging Elements and Packaging Composition descriptions for all languages required for the Declaration of Conformity (DoC)
  • Compliance status at Packaging Elements and Packaging Composition level for PPWR Article 5.4 and 5.5
  • Product Contact information for Packaging Elements

Note: Integration can be updated with SAP Note 3705388.

2. Centralized Packaging Compliance Documentation

SAP Responsible Design and Production now enables customers to:

  • Store test reports, audit documents, and certificates
  • Link supporting evidence directly to packaging elements
  • Maintain all compliance content in a single system

This ensures that Declarations of Conformity are backed by auditable evidence, improving trust and regulatory readiness.

3. Ready-to-Use Declaration of Conformity (DoC) Templates

To accelerate compliance readiness:

  • Preconfigured DoC templates are now available out-of-the-box, go here for more details
  • Supported in multiple languages (EN, DE, FR, ES, IT, NL)
  • Coming soon*: Fully customizable templates to meet company-specific needs

These templates significantly reduce effort and provide a fast path to generating compliant documentation.

4. Conformity Assessment & DoC Generation

A key innovation is the ability to:

  • Analyze and review packaging data in a consolidated view
  • Identify missing compliance information
  • Generate Declarations of Conformity efficiently

This end‑to‑end flow allows customers to move from raw data to validated compliance output at SKU level.

 

5. Coming Soon*: Packaging Data Collaboration

We recognise that critical packaging data often resides with suppliers. A new capability in Responsible Design and Production will soon enable companies to request and receive this data directly from suppliers, supported by integration with SAP Sustainability Data Exchange (SDX).

This will help customers bridge data gaps and improve data completeness—a key challenge highlighted by many organizations.

What’s Next

SAP will continue to evolve PPWR capabilities beyond the initial 2026 requirements, providing a future-proof compliance solution.. Planned enhancements include: 

  • Expanded compliance coverage for upcoming PPWR articles
  • Updates to Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR) reports
  • Advanced analytics and dashboards
  • AI-assisted data gap detection and completion recommendations

Stay tuned, more updates will follow as these capabilities become available.

* Please refer to SAP Roadmap Explorer for delivery timelines, SAP Roadmap disclaimer applies.

entry.title: Re: Requirement for GRN in Different Plant While Invoice...
entry.content:
Good day @S0027122092 

Thank you for your question.

There are two separate options, depending on your intended inventory ownership.

Option 1: Is for you to selectively deactivate QM for specific materials in plant 1200

Yes, this is possible. Quality inspection settings are maintained at material and plant level. Therefore, inspection type 01 for goods receipt from purchasing can remain active for some materials in plant 1200 and be inactive for others.

Here are references for more guidance

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/f86dc2eb1f8b48c880a7607213104b27/22606eb7dac24327b72a31c1dea11f7b.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/3c916ef10fc240c9afc594b346ffaf77/bcea6b94b9b84138b7dcf3b932cbc02b.html?locale=en-US

 

For the affected material:

  1. Open Manage Product Master Data.                                                         
  2. Select the material and plant 1200
  3. Open the Quality Management data.
  4. Review the active inspection type for goods receipt from purchasing.
  5. Deactivate the inspection type only if the material should no longer generate inspection lots.
  6. Also check the Post to Inspection Stock setting.

This should ensure goods receipt for that material is posted to unrestricted use stock rather than quality inspection stock, provided no other stock relevant inspection type remains active.

This is only appropriate when plant 1200 remain the inventory and valuation plant.

 

Option 2: Is for you to post the physical receipt and inventory directly in plant 1300

A purchase order item you created for plant 1200 cannot normally be received as inventory in plant 1300. Entering plant 1300 as the delivery address changes only the physical delivery address. It does not change the receiving plant in the purchasing and material documents.

If plant 1300 is the actual inventory receiving location, I recommend you follow the cleaner standard design, which is:

  • Create the purchase oder item from plant 1300
  • Post goods receipt directly in plant 1300
  • Post the supplier invoice with reference to that purchase order.
  • Maintain QM setting for the material specifically in plant 1300.

I would suggest you should use business role, company code, purchasing organisation, tax, or custom process restriction to review your requirement that supplier invoices can only be posted for purchase orders belonging to plant 1200.

If plant 1200 and 1300 belong to different company codes, the legal purchasing and inventory ownership design must also be confirmed. A stock transfer order may be required where one plant purchases the material and subsequently transfers ownership or stock to the other plant.

See the following reference for more insights.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/32da8359c8ee4e8b8e8c5e15cacba5aa/9ddf815494758c4ce10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/38cbf557c328be12e10000000a4450e5.html?locale=en-US

 

To summarise:

You can selectively deactivate QM by material and plant. However, changing the delivery address does not permit a purchase order for plant 1200 to post inventor directly into plant 1300. The recommended approach is either to keep the PO and receipt in plant 1200 and adjust the material specific QM settings or create the PO for plant 1300 when that plant is the true inventory recipient.

 

 

Let me know if you have further question.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: 5 Personalization Mistakes That Ruin Customer Experience
entry.content:
Key Takeaways 

  • Personalization should be continuous and ongoing. Build richer customer profiles over time through progressive profiling. 
  • Use real-time signals from both customers and your business to drive engagement. Event-triggered personalization outperforms static campaigns. 
  • Customers expect seamless, relevant experiences from the very first interaction, even before they identify themselves or opt in. 
  • Focus your personalization on key moments that influence decisions, such as product availability, onboarding, or cart abandonment. 
  • Personalization should always guide action, driving measurable outcomes like conversions, retention, and revenue. 

Quick answers 

What are the most common personalization mistakes in marketing? 

The most common personalization mistakes include treating personalization as a one-time data collection effort, ignoring real-time business signals, delivering disjointed omnichannel experiences, neglecting anonymous users, and failing to tie personalization to measurable business outcomes. 

Why is one-time data collection ineffective for personalization? 

One-time data collection quickly becomes outdated and can overwhelm customers upfront. To effectively personalize messages, marketers should apply progressive profiling strategies and continuously update customer data through ongoing interactions, dynamically updating customer profiles and segmentation based on the customer’s behavioral signals. 

How can brands use real-time signals to improve personalization? 

Brands can improve personalization by using event-driven marketing powered by real-time signals from both customers (e.g., browsing behavior) and internal systems (e.g., inventory or supply chain data). By connecting data sources and actioning that data with AI, organizations enable instant campaign adjustments. 

~~~~~ 

When it comes to personalized customer engagement, every brand is in the process of development. A “personalization mistake” doesn’t spell tragedy—instead, it’s an opportunity to redefine processes and potentially crack open new revenue streams.  

One true sign of marketing maturity is the ability to take basic or outdated strategies, infuse them with relevant customer and operational data, and then deliver messages that are relevant, meaningful, and timely. 

Let’s explore five common personalization “mistakes” and how you can steer your brand toward customer engagement maturity using practical examples from our Personalization Playbook, Second Edition.  

1. Treating personalization as a one-time data grab 

The mindset that personalization depends on a one-time data grab can lead to multiple missteps, such as... 

  • Asking for too much information up front, overwhelming the customer with questions that feel unnatural and invasive 
  • Using outdated information about the customer later on, after their needs and preferences have changed 

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Your customers are dynamic and always changing, so the way you engage them should also be dynamic. It’s important to use progressive profiling tactics to get to know your customers over time. Focus on earning data through repeat interactions, continuously updating customer profiles. Real-time behavior signals like category browsing, purchase, and channel usage all add up to create a bigger picture of who your customer is and what motivates them. 

Be sure to explain how the data will be used and then use the data in ways that benefit not just your brand, but the customer—this goes a long way toward building trust and long-term loyalty. 

Earn that data; don’t just hoard it.  

Quick tips  

  • Use a participation check, validating that the audience is willing to opt in to the experience. This helps ensure you’ve identified the best target audience. 
  • Offer a value exchange that gives your customer a reason to jump at the chance to opt in. 
  • Enable digital ads to adapt in real time with Google BigQuery and AI to leverage live ERP signals.  

Playbook example 

In this series of mobile interactions, the brand gradually gathers data over time (Personalization Playbook: page 12). 

 2. Missing your own real-time signals 

Marketers talk a lot about customer signals, especially ones that show intent to purchase.  

Not all signals come from the customer! Many come from within your own organization, such as service, supply chain, finance, sales, etc. Failure to act on these real-time signals results in missed opportunities.  

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Your brand needs to react in the moment, not hours or days after a significant event that impacts customer experience. Personalization must be event-driven, not calendar-driven.  

However, human beings can’t possibly react to every signal in real time. That's where AI comes in—when your data is unified and your customer engagement solution is connected with your Enterprise Resource Platform (ERP), AI can dynamically adapt campaigns to events as they happen. Yet 78% of brands can’t practice real-time AI optimization in day-to-day campaigns (Global Engagement Index, 2026). As customer expectations rise and AI capabilities become more widespread, brands will need to innovate or risk falling behind competitors. 

Quick tips 

  • Your supply chain should inform your messaging. You can create urgency around low stock, excitement about back-in-stock products, or awareness about new products that pair well with prior purchases.  
  • Use customer wish lists and waiting lists to target high-intent customers with the right messaging the moment products become available.  
  • Personalize SMS messages with the name of the back-in-stock product and a link to explore or purchase. 

Playbook example 

The following campaign flow shows how you can inform customers via email that an item is back in stock, give product recommendations, and send an SMS message about it (Personalization Playbook, page 26). 

 3. Personalizing in siloes instead of omnichannel 

Your customers don’t care that your channels are all in siloes—to them, there is only one journey with your brand, and that journey is happening on whatever channel they use. Disjointed messages across channels feels inconsistent and confusing to customers.  

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Channels should work in harmony to tell your brand story in a cohesive, consistent way. That doesn’t mean copy-pasting the same message across channels, using each channel to its best advantage (i.e. SMS for time-sensitive messages, email for newsletters, etc. 

Quick tips  

  • Unified customer profiles help organize customer data to reveal patterns and trends, enabling you to deliver personalized content based on customer behaviors.  
  • Using consistent personalization tokens throughout your content helps ensure that messaging is consistent and relevant.  
  • AI can help identify and build target audience segments based on criteria you set (channel opt-in, average order value, category engagement, etc.). 

Playbook example 

Use email and conversational channels to launch a new product. AI-driven channel engagement scoring can help you target the segments that are most likely to engage on different channels (Personalization Playbook, page 22). 

 4. Ignoring anonymous and early-stage customers 

Don’t wait on a written invitation from the customer to personalize content for them. If you’re personalizing content only for known contacts and neglecting your anonymous visitors, you may be waiting too long and missing the chance to guide early-stage customers down the funnel. 

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Personalization can (and should) begin even before identification. Here’s what that can look like:  

  • Welcome message: Give the customer the option to choose a guided introduction to your brand and offerings.  
  • Behavior-driven targeting: Make offers and recommendations based on the customer’s browsing behavior, such as interest in a specific category, clicks on clearance items, or selecting certain sizes or colors. 
  • Smart overlays: Use web pops and other CTAs to make relevant offers, convert online shoppers to in-store traffic, recommend products, etc. 

Quick tips 

  • Invite new web visitors to sign up for your newsletter or loyalty program—let them know right away what value you’re offering for their participation. 
  • After capturing an email address, trigger an automated Welcome campaign for immediate follow-up. 
  • Personalize digital ads to redirect leads to content that’s related to the categories they browsed.  

Playbook example 

Give customers a chance to opt-in to a more tailored, guided experience, and then follow up by connecting the customer with a sales representative, converting qualified leads faster (Personalization Playbook, page 16). 

 5. Forgetting that personalization must drive action 

At the end of the day, you aren’t delivering personalized experiences just for the sake of it—you do it to give customers the tailored experiences they deserve and also to drive real, tangible results for your brand.  

Superficial personalization doesn’t help customers make decisions and doesn’t drive revenue. 

How to demonstrate marketing maturity 

Focus on triggered, outcome-driven automations that drive conversion, retention, and re-engagement.  

Personalization must be:  

  • Contextual, based on what the customer is doing (events like abandoned cart, searching your blog, reading reviews) 
  • Action-oriented, guiding the customer’s next action (complete the cart checkout, purchase the item in the blog, discover a key product testimonial) 
  • Measurable, based on conversion, revenue, and retention 

Quick tips 

  • Ensure your customer engagement solution has visibility into both customer and operational (ERP) data.  
  • Use ERP data to target both anonymous and known customers and trigger follow-ups on events.   
  • For measuring outcomes, use AI to build and deliver reports on a scheduled basis. 

Playbook example 

In this example of an abandoned cart notification, personalization tokens make the message specific and actionable for the customer (Personalization Playbook, page 40). 

Closing thoughts: Mistakes are stepping stones toward marketing maturity 

Personalization "mistakes” rarely come from a lack of effort—they come from outdated strategies. By moving away from static campaigns and surface-level tactics and toward contextual, timely engagement you can advance to the next level of marketing maturity. 

Dive deeper and get more plays! 

Personalization Playbook, Second Edition

entry.title: We are experiencing an issue regarding batch classification validation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public
entry.content:
Dear Support Team,

We are experiencing an issue regarding batch classification validation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

Current Configuration:

  1. We have created a characteristic and flagged it as a Required Entry.
  2. This characteristic is assigned to a Batch Class (Class Type 023), where it is also marked as a Required Entry (Class Overwrite).
  3. The Batch Class is assigned to the Material Master record.

The Issue:
When creating a Production Order (CO01) and generating a batch under the Goods Receipt tab, the standard Batch Classification dialog pop-up appears as expected. However, the system allows the user to completely bypass the mandatory characteristic entry. The user can close the pop-up or process past it without entering a value, and the system saves the production order.

Since the characteristic is explicitly flagged as a Required Entry, the system should enforce a hard block (Error) rather than a by passable Information.

We have also tested the scenario in CO11N. However, the system behavior remains the same.

Our Questions:

  1. Is there any standard configuration available in the Public Cloud edition to enforce a hard validation error for incomplete batch classification during order creation/release?
  2. If this is standard behaviour at the order creation stage, could you please provide the exact technical name of the cloud-ready BAdI (Business Add-In) or Enhancement Spot available to validate batch classification data upon saving the production order?

I have attached screenshots of our characteristic/class configuration and the order creation screen for your reference.

 

Regards, 

Shubham

entry.title: Simplifying EBRR Job Scheduling with Company Code and Ledger Ranges
entry.content:
When running Event-Based Revenue Recognition (EBRR) at period-end, many teams still think in terms of one job per company code or one job per ledger.

But there’s a simpler way.

  • EBRR application jobs allow you to process ranges of company codes and ledgers in a single run.

Period-end closing often involves:

  • Multiple company codes
  • Multiple ledgers
  • Repetitive job scheduling

Instead of managing this complexity manually, you can define everything upfront—and let the system handle the rest.

 
When you schedule a job with ranges, the system:
  • Takes your selected company codes and ledgers
  • Builds all possible combinations
  • Validates each combination
  • Processes only the valid ones

How you define the scope

1. Manual Entry

  • Enter lists of company codes and ledgers directly

2. Smart Selection (F4 Help)

  • Search using conditions
  • Filter by attributes
  • Build dynamic selections

 

Where You Can Use It

This capability is available across all EBRR period-end jobs, including:

  • Run Revenue Recognition - Projects (F4277)
  • Run Revenue Recognition - Sales Orders (F4276)
  • Run Revenue Recognition - Service Documents (F4278)
  • Run Revenue Recognition - Provider Contracts (F5405)
  • Run Revenue Recognition - Revenue Contracts (F7347)

entry.title: SAP TM: Allocating Truck Operating Costs to Freight Orders
entry.content:
Environment: SAP S/4HANA 2023 Embedded TM

Business Requirement

The customer operates a fleet of trucks and incurs various vehicle-related expenses such as:

  • Fuel
  • Maintenance
  • Insurance
  • Depreciation

These costs are posted to expense accounts or truck-related cost centers and are not currently assigned to individual Freight Orders.

The customer would like to allocate these indirect truck costs to the Freight Orders that utilized the corresponding truck resource, in order to determine the actual cost and profitability of each trip.

Question

Is there a standard SAP TM solution that allows Freight Orders to receive allocated costs from cost centers or expense accounts? If not, Is there is an official sap note indicate that ?

Can a Freight Order act as a cost object (similar to an Internal Order) for assessment/distribution cycles, or is a custom allocation approach required to distribute truck expenses across Freight Orders based on usage? if so, could you provide it ?

entry.title: Re: Issue with Automatic Clearing by Ignoring Partial Rec...
entry.content:
Hi R_Pooja,

Thanks for the details. I ran some further tests on this, and the cause isn't Posting Rule F001 or Algorithm 021 — it's the "Include Invoice Reference" editing option.

This setting is per user. When it's off, the system ignores the partial-payment link and clears the invoice on its own; when it's on, it pulls the linked partial payment into the same group, so the balance no longer matches the new full receipt and the invoice is no longer auto-cleared.

The key point: it has to be active on the user who actually posts the clearing with your processing rule (often a technical/background user for automated EBS), not just on the analyst's own account. So please check which user runs the rule and switch the option on for that user — in Edit User-Specific Options for Journal Entries → Open Items tab. It also applies to partial payments already posted.

BR, Michal

entry.title: Re: Automatic Calculation/Posting of Goodwill in Grp Repo...
entry.content:
Hi Kashmira,

The goodwill is not automatically posted as such.

The differential is automatically posted on retained earnings. This is because the system cannot decide that this differential is a Goodwill that can be activated. This needs a business rationale and decision, e.g. is there a value from an acquisition that can be activated. This is why reporting the recognized goodwill value, with a manual journal against the retained earnings reducing the differential from the automatic postings, is needed.

This is the rules we deliver in our Group Reporeting content, but customers can decide differently and configure their rules accordingly.

Hope this helps.

Grégoire

Govern before you scale.

entry.title: Re: Integration between S/4HANA Cloud and Integration Suite with SOAP Outbound service using Output
entry.content:
@Arnab_G 

SOAP outbound from S/4HANA Cloud to Integration Suite:

  • Define outbound SOAP service in S/4HANA Cloud

  • In Integration Suite: SOAP sender channel + endpoint URL + security (cert/WS-Security) + mapping

  • Add retries and dedup keys to avoid duplicates

  • Monitor Integration Suite runtime and S/4 logs for failures

SOAP is reliable but heavy. Use REST/OData or events when possible.

entry.title: CK11N Picking Negative Procurement Price from Info Record (No PO Involved)
entry.content:
While executing cost estimate using CK11N, the system is determining a negative procurement price for a material.

  • The system correctly identifies the Purchasing Info Record (no Purchase Order is being picked).
  • However, the calculated price is negative, although:
    • The Info Record shows a positive price in ME13.
    • There is no PO involved in the valuation.
  • Valuation Variant:
    • Strategy: L – Price from Purchasing Info Record
    • Sub-strategy: B – Purchase Order Price via Condition Table
  • latest PO is return PO

entry.title: Inconsistent Behavior of SAP Build Work Zone Fiori Apps on Mobile (Joule Work App & Browser Access)?
entry.content:
Introduction

In our recent implementation of the My Time Sheet application via SAP Build Work Zone, we conducted multiple testing sessions after enabling external (internet) access.

While the application performs well in controlled environments, we observed inconsistent and non-deterministic behavior across mobile devices, browsers, and the Joule Work app. In some cases, the application works seamlessly, while in others, it results in partial loading or blank screens.

This article summarizes our findings and highlights key architectural considerations for the SAP community.

Architecture Overview

To provide full context, the solution is implemented using a standard SAP architecture with the following components:

  • Authentication: Azure Active Directory (Azure AD)
  • Authorization & Trust: SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
  • Frontend Runtime: SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)
  • Mobile Access: Joule Work App
  • Backend Connectivity: SAP Web Dispatcher with Principal Propagation
  • Application Type: HTML5 / SAPUI5 (Fiori-based)

End-to-End Flow (Simplified)

Mobile / Browser
      ↓
Azure AD (Authentication)
      ↓
IAS (Trust & Authorization)
      ↓
SAP Build Work Zone (HTML5 App)
      ↓
Destination Service
      ↓
Web Dispatcher (Principal Propagation)
      ↓
Backend (e.g., S/4HANA)

This architecture introduces multiple authentication hops, session dependencies, and runtime layers, which may contribute to instability in mobile scenarios.

Testing Scope

Testing was performed across:

  • iOS devices (work & personal)
  • Android devices (work & personal profiles)
  • Work and private laptops (Chrome & Edge)
  • Access methods:
    • Direct URL (browser)
    • Joule Work mobile application

Observed Behavior

1. iOS (iPhone)

Browser Access

  • ✅ Works in Microsoft Edge
  • ✅ Works in Google app
  • ❌ Not working in Chrome

Joule Work App

  • ❌ App opens but remains blank / non-functional

2. Android

Browser Access

  • ✅ Works consistently

Joule Work App

  • ✅ Initially works
  • ⚠️ Stops working after some time (session inconsistency)

3. Desktop (Laptops)

  • ✅ Works reliably in Chrome
  • ⚠️ In Microsoft Edge, tiles sometimes fail to load initially but work after refresh

Key Issues Identified

1. Inconsistent Behavior

  • Same application behaves differently:
    • Across devices (iOS vs Android)
    • Across browsers (Edge vs Chrome)
    • Across sessions (intermittent failures)

2. Blank Screen Issue

  • Observed in both:
    • Browser (after successful launch)
    • Joule Work app (application shell loads, but content is empty)

3. Strong Browser Dependency

  • Chrome on iOS consistently fails
  • Edge and Google app perform better

4. Mobile App Instability (Joule Work)

  • iOS: Not working
  • Android: Works intermittently

Architectural Observations

Based on the current setup, the issue appears to be architectural rather than purely configuration-related.

1. Multi-Layer Authentication Complexity

  • Authentication via Azure AD
  • Trust and session handling via IAS
  • Backend access via Principal Propagation (Web Dispatcher)

This creates:

  • Multiple token exchanges
  • Session dependencies across layers
  • Increased chances of session misalignment

2. Session Handling in Mobile Environments

  • SAP Build Work Zone relies on:
    • Browser-based sessions
    • Cookies and storage

Mobile platforms (especially iOS):

  • Restrict cookies (SameSite policies)
  • Isolate sessions in WebView containers

Result:

  • Session loss
  • Token expiration not handled gracefully
  • Blank screen scenarios

3. Joule Work App Limitations

  • Likely uses a WebView container
  • WebView environments:
    • Do not fully share authentication context with browser sessions

Particularly problematic in:

  • Multi-hop authentication (Azure AD → IAS → Work Zone)

4. Distributed Runtime Dependencies

Application flow includes:

  • Work Zone
  • HTML5 App Repository
  • Destination Service
  • Web Dispatcher
  • Backend system

👉 Any intermittent failure in this chain:

  • Results in incomplete UI rendering
  • No clear error feedback to user

5. iOS Platform Constraints

  • All browsers use WebKit
  • Strict enforcement of:
    • Cookie handling
    • Session persistence

 Leading to:

  • Chrome not working
  • Inconsistent app behavior

Conclusion

While the application performs well under stable conditions, the current setup shows:

  • ❌ Inconsistent behavior across devices and browsers
  • ❌ Limited reliability for mobile usage
  • ⚠️ High dependency on session handling across multiple layers

This makes the solution not fully stable for productive mobile usage at this stage.

Questions to the SAP Community

I would appreciate insights on the following:

  1. Is this behavior a known limitation when using:

    • Azure AD + IAS
    • Work Zone + Joule Work app
    • Principal Propagation via Web Dispatcher

  2. Is Joule Work app officially recommended for:

    • Work Zone HTML5/Fiori apps in such architectures?

  3. What is the recommended mobile strategy?

    • SAP Mobile Start?
    • SAP MDK-based applications?

  1. Are there best practices for:

    • Stabilizing session handling across Azure AD and IAS?
    • Avoiding blank screen issues in Work Zone apps?

  2. Has anyone implemented a similar architecture successfully with consistent mobile behavior?

Final Thought

This experience highlights an important architectural insight:

SAP Build Work Zone works reliably in browser-based environments, but mobile usage—especially with external authentication providers and multi-hop architectures—requires careful alignment with SAP’s recommended mobile strategy.

entry.title: We are experiencing an issue regarding batch classification validation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public
entry.content:
Dear Support Team, 

We are experiencing an issue regarding batch classification validation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.  

Current Configuration: 

  1. We have created a characteristic and flagged it as a Required Entry. 
  1. This characteristic is assigned to a Batch Class (Class Type 023), where it is also marked as a Required Entry (Class Overwrite). 
  1. The Batch Class is assigned to the Material Master record.  

The Issue: 
When creating a Production Order (CO01) and generating a batch under the Goods Receipt tab, the standard Batch Classification dialog pop-up appears as expected. However, the system allows the user to completely bypass the mandatory characteristic entry. The user can close the pop-up or process past it without entering a value, and the system saves the production order. 

Since the characteristic is explicitly flagged as a Required Entry, the system should enforce a hard block (Error) rather than a by passable Information. 

We have also tested the scenario in CO11N. However, the system behavior remains the same. 

Our Questions: 

  1. Is there any standard configuration available in the Public Cloud edition to enforce a hard validation error for incomplete batch classification during order creation/release? 
  1. If this is standard behaviour at the order creation stage, could you please provide the exact technical name of the cloud-ready BAdI (Business Add-In) or Enhancement Spot available to validate batch classification data upon saving the production order? 

I have attached screenshots of our characteristic/class configuration and the order creation screen for your reference.

Regards,

Shubham

entry.title: Locking Product Master Fields: Protecting Your Product Data
entry.content:
Managing Product Master Data Field Locking

Managing product master data requires careful control, especially when certain information needs to remain unchanged. SAP's field locking functionality provides a robust solution for protecting critical product data from unintended modifications. 

What Is Field Locking? 

Field locking allows you to designate specific fields in your product master records as "lock-relevant." Once a product master record is locked, these protected fields cannot be modified through the Manage Product Master Data app. This safeguard activates immediately when you save the record. 

 Getting Started 

 To use this feature, your business role needs the catalog SAP_CMD_BC_PR_DIGITAL_LOCK_PC. Alternatively, you can create a role using the template SAP_BR_PRODMASTER_SPECIALIST, which includes the necessary authorization. Your system administrator must also grant you permission to lock (and in exceptional cases, unlock) product master records. 

 Configuring Lock-Relevant Fields 

 SAP provides two configuration activities for defining which fields should be lock-relevant: 

Define Lock-Relevant Fields (OMSFIX): This activity works with existing material master fields for industry. You can enable or disable lock-relevance for these fields, but you cannot add new ones. In test systems, you have flexibility to change settings anytime. However, in production systems, you can only remove lock-relevance if no locked materials exist. 
To access the activity, use SPRO transaction and navigate to Logistics - General > Material Master > Field Selection > Define Lock Relevant Fields.

Define Lock-Relevant Fields in Product Master (OMSFIXC): This enhanced activity offers more flexibility—you can add new entries and enable lock-relevance for additional fields. Changes made here take precedence over OMSFIX settings, except for extensible fields. 

  • In SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition: To access the activity, use SPRO transaction and navigate to Logistics - General > Material Master > Field Selection > Define Lock Relevant Fields in Product Master.
  • In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: To access the configuration step, go to Database Management > Product > Define Lock-Relevant Fields in Product Master in your configuration environment.

Note: Both OMSFIXC and OMSFIX are available in SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition. Only OMSFIXC is available in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 

Working with Locked Products 

 Locking a product is straightforward: simply select the "Product is Locked" checkbox under Basic Data in the Manage Product Master Data app.  

 Need to make changes to a locked product? With proper authorization, you can temporarily unlock the record, make your edits, then re-lock it before saving. However, remember that once locked, products typically cannot be unlocked unless you have special authorization and the product isn't used in configuration management. 

 Important Considerations 

 Keep these points in mind when implementing field locking: 

  • By default, no fields are lock-relevant in the standard system—you must configure this yourself 
  • If you flag a field as lock-relevant after scheduling changes for that field in a locked record, those scheduled changes will be lost 
  • This functionality applies only to product master for industry, not retail 
  • The lock takes effect only after saving the record 

 Field locking is a powerful tool for maintaining data integrity in your product master records. By carefully selecting which fields to protect and establishing clear authorization protocols, you can prevent accidental changes while maintaining the flexibility your business needs. 

 Have you implemented field locking in your SAP environment? What challenges or benefits have you experienced? I'd love to hear about your experiences in the comments below.

entry.title: SAP BW Modernization and Stewardship with SAP Business Data Cloud - Introduction
entry.content:
Lift - Shift - Innovate - Govern - Decommission

This is the introduction blog of a series that covers the phases of the SAP BW modernization journey. 

For over two decades, SAP Business Warehouse (SAP BW) has served as the analytical backbone of enterprises, supporting financial reporting, operational intelligence, and regulatory compliance. Many continue to rely on SAP BW 7.5, SAP BW powered by SAP HANA, and SAP BW/4HANA to consolidate and govern mission-critical business data. 

However, the conditions under which SAP BW was originally designed have changed significantly. Enterprises now operate in a data environment characterized by larger volumes, faster decision cycles, broader integration requirements, and increased pressure to support advanced analytics and artificial intelligence. Business users no longer expect only structured historical reporting. They expect near-real-time insight, self-service access to trusted data, cross-domain semantic consistency, and support for predictive and AI-driven decision-making. At the same time, IT organizations are under constant pressure to reduce total cost of ownership, simplify operations, improve governance, and modernize aging data platforms before maintenance deadlines create forced decisions. 

These pressures are not independent. They reinforce each other. Rising infrastructure costs make it harder to justify on-premises investment. The shift to AI-ready architectures highlights the limitations of legacy modeling. The maintenance horizon places a hard upper limit on how long delay is feasible. Together, they make clear that modernization is no longer optional. The only question is how to execute it. 

This is the context in which SAP BW Modernization and Stewardship has become a strategic priority - not merely a technology upgrade, but a deliberate repositioning of the enterprise data platform for a new era. 

SAP Business Data Cloud: From Monolithic Data Warehouse to Business Data Fabric 

The architectural shift is significant. Traditional BW landscapes are centralized by design. They combine ingestion, transformation, persistence, semantic logic, and reporting in a tightly coupled model. That design brought control and consistency, but it also created bottlenecks. Over time, many enterprises found that custom logic, data duplication and silos expanded continuously, while analytical agility declined. 

SAP BDC introduces a much different architecture and philosophy. Enterprises are guided away from rigid, monolithic data warehouses toward a business data fabric and a decentralized data mesh. BW assets can be retained, exposed, modernized, governed, and eventually retired in a phased approach: Lift, Shift, Innovate, Govern, and Decommission, avoiding a high-risk big bang migration. The main phases are Lift (1), Shift (2) and Innovate (3) complemented by Govern (4) and finally Decommission (5). 

All phases form both a sequence of activities and the structure of this blog series. 

  1. Lift - Establish the foundation: Migrate SAP BW / BW4HANA on-premises to SAP BDC (SAP BW PCE) as-is to establish a stable foundation without disrupting current operations. 
  2. Shift - Begin the modernization journey: Publish SAP BW data as governed data products within SAP Business Data Cloud, making it reusable. 
  3. Innovate - Create an AI ready data platform: Adopt SAP-managed data products, intelligent applications, and cloud-native analytical patterns, replacing legacy SAP BW-centered use cases. 
  4. Govern - Ensure data integrity, compliance and user trust: Establish the stewardship controls for a sustainable data product operating model. 
  5. Decommission - Gradually retire SAP BW until it’s no longer required. 

These phases are not strictly linear. Different business domains may move through them at different speeds. Some may remain in coexistence for a while, while others modernize quickly. Governance spans the entire journey, and decommissioning should be planned early even if executed later. 

If you're interested to start the BW modernization journey follow this blog series and get your free assessment.
